papers.title: BitBypass: Jailbreaking Large Language Models via Bitstream Camouflage
papers.annotation: New jailbreak vector. Encodes adversarial payloads as bitstreams to bypass alignment filters. Direct implications for Shield detection patterns.

article.deck: Adversarial payloads encoded as bitstreams circumvent alignment filters entirely — a new vector class with direct implications for Shield detection architecture
body: BitBypass presents a jailbreak methodology that operates below the token level. By encoding adversarial intent as bitstream-camouflaged payloads, the attack surface shifts from semantic content to structural encoding — a layer most current alignment filters do not inspect. The model processes what appears to be benign input while executing the embedded instruction.

The architecture of this attack is significant precisely because it is invisible to surface-level content moderation. Standard toxicity classifiers, prompt injection detectors, and even chain-of-thought monitors operate at the semantic layer. BitBypass sidesteps that layer entirely. The question this raises for defensive systems is whether inspection needs to descend to the encoding layer — and what the performance cost of that descent would be in production environments.

Sword implications are immediate: this is a viable evasion technique for any agent operating under monitored conditions. Shield implications are more complex: detection requires rethinking what constitutes the content of a prompt.

papers.title: Learning When to Act or Refuse: Guarding Agentic Reasoning with Reinforcement Learning
papers.annotation: Multi-step tool-use guardrails via RL. Maps to Sword evasion and Shield prompt injection defense.

article.deck: Reinforcement learning applied to multi-step tool-use guardrails — mapping the boundary between autonomous action and required refusal across agentic task chains
body: The core problem in agentic systems is not whether a model can be told to refuse — it is whether a model knows when to refuse without being told. This paper applies RL to learn refusal timing across multi-step tool-use chains, where a single wrong action mid-sequence can cascade into irreversible outcomes.

The methodology is relevant in both directions. For Shield, it offers a framework for building refusal into the reasoning chain rather than bolting it on at output. For Sword, the learned refusal patterns become the map of where evasion is possible — the gaps between steps where a multi-turn injection can redirect the agent before the guardrail fires.

The paper's treatment of irreversibility thresholds is particularly applicable to the self-evolving system: at what point in an autonomous loop does a wrong action become unrecoverable, and can that point be learned rather than hard-coded?

papers.title: The Synthetic Web: Adversarial Mini-Internets for Diagnosing Language Model Epistemic Weaknesses
papers.annotation: Adversarially-curated environments to diagnose epistemic weaknesses. Potential Experiment 3 methodology.

article.deck: Adversarially-curated information environments as a methodology for diagnosing epistemic weaknesses in language model reasoning — potential framework for Experiment 3
body: This paper constructs what it calls adversarial mini-internets: small, coherent information environments where the content is specifically designed to induce epistemic failure in a target model. The model navigates what appears to be a plausible web of sources, each reinforcing the others, all pointing toward a false conclusion.

The construction methodology — seeding contradictions, manufacturing citation chains, deploying authority mimicry at scale — maps directly onto influence operation architecture. The difference is that here it is used diagnostically: to find where a model's epistemic immune system fails. The same technique, applied offensively, is a cognitive environment construction attack.

For the self-evolving system, this raises the question of whether the scanner itself is susceptible to synthetic web poisoning — whether a sufficiently well-constructed adversarial paper corpus could redirect the taxonomy evolution in a chosen direction without triggering detection.
